summary: I was born in Hiroshima Ken on April 5th, 1899, and grew up in a farming family near Hiroshima city. Our home was only about two ri, or five miles, from Hiroshima itself, so I was very close to the city and the military headquarters there during the Russo-Japanese War. Living in Hiroshima Prefecture shaped much of my early life, from attending local festivals to experiencing the presence of soldiers who stayed with families nearby.

My mother often welcomed Komusō, the strolling flute players who were usually Buddhist monks, into our home to stay overnight. These monks, along with other traveling priests, were treated with great kindness by my mother. As a child, I saw many people come to talk to her in whispers, and some of them would cry, which puzzled me at the time. My mother’s hospitality to Komusō showed her generous and caring nature.

I was raised in a Buddhist family, and our main temple was Nishihonganji. My mother was very religious and sent me to a Buddhist Sunday school held on Saturday nights, where I learned to recite Okyo by heart. I also attended sermons by Buddhist priests and was taught traditional arts like tea ceremony and flower arrangement at the temple from a young age. Although I didn’t fully understand the teachings then, Buddhism was an important part of my upbringing.

Our family’s main temple was Nishi Hongan-ji, which played a significant role in my religious education. It was there that I attended Sunday school and learned traditional arts like tea ceremony and flower arrangement. The temple was a place of spiritual learning and community gatherings during my childhood in Hiroshima Prefecture.

When I was a small child, a white man came to our town and showed us magic lantern slides telling the story of Jesus Christ. One story that stayed with me was about the Samaritan woman giving water to Jesus, where Jesus said those who drink from him will never thirst. Even though I was raised Buddhist, that story of Jesus deeply touched my heart and left a lasting impression on me. I often wanted to see that man again when I saw him in town.

I remember the story shown by a white evangelist about the Samaritan woman who gave water to Jesus. This story was part of a magic lantern slide presentation in my hometown, and it made a strong impression on me as a child. Although I was raised Buddhist, the message from Samaria’s story stayed with me deeply.

From a very young age, I was taught the tea ceremony at the Buddhist temple near our home. It was part of the traditional education for young women, alongside flower arrangement and sewing. Learning tea ceremony helped me respect the Buddhist priests and appreciate our cultural heritage during my childhood in Hiroshima Prefecture.

Like the tea ceremony, flower arrangement was taught to me at the temple when I was a child. It was considered an essential skill for young women and was part of my upbringing in a religious and traditional household. These lessons connected me to the cultural practices of our community in Hiroshima Prefecture.

During local festivals and special occasions, my parents would dress me in a very fine kimono, which made those outings especially memorable. Wearing a kimono was part of the traditional culture I grew up with in Hiroshima Prefecture. I remember feeling proud and happy when I wore it to festivals and went out with our family friends.

I remember one of my school teachers came from Shimane Ken. She was very strict but also kind, and I recall her very well from my schooling years. Although I was born and raised in Hiroshima Prefecture, this teacher from Shimane left a strong impression on me during my education.

When I was in the second grade of Koto Shogakko, we celebrated Chikyo Setsu, the birthday of the Empress. We brought food and money to school and had a communal meal and a talent show, which was very enjoyable and memorable for me. This event also made me aware of social divisions, as a classmate from the Shin Hei Min group did not participate because others would not accept her.

At school, I encountered children from the Shin Hei Min, or new citizens, who were considered the lowest class of people. I used to be mean to one girl from that group without understanding how much it hurt her. After she told me she couldn’t join celebrations because others would not like her, I felt very sorry and stopped treating them badly. This experience made me realize the harsh social discrimination they faced.

I clearly remember the Nichiro Sensō, or Russo-Japanese War, when I was about six or seven years old. At that time, the Fifth Division of the Imperial Japanese Army was headquartered in Hiroshima, very close to my home. Soldiers from all over Japan stayed with local families, including ours, and my mother took great care of them. We often went to the railroad station to wave flags and cheer 'Banzai' as the soldiers left for battle.

During the Russo-Japanese War, the Fifth Division of the Imperial Japanese Army was stationed in Hiroshima, just a few miles from my home. Many soldiers from this division stayed with local families, including mine, and my mother cared for them kindly. I remember seeing them march off at night and holding paper lanterns with our family crest as we said goodbye.

The Imperial Japanese Army was very present in my hometown during the Russo-Japanese War, with the Fifth Division headquartered nearby. Soldiers from all over Japan came to Hiroshima and stayed with local families, including mine. My mother treated these soldiers with great kindness, and we often went to the station to see them off, waving flags and shouting 'Banzai'.

Hiroshima was very close to my home, only about two ri away, and it influenced much of my childhood. During the Russo-Japanese War, the military headquarters were there, and soldiers stayed with families in the surrounding areas. I remember going to the railroad station in Hiroshima to wave flags and cheer the soldiers as they left for war.

During the time of the Russo-Japanese War, we children would go to the railroad station every day to wave flags and shout 'Banzai' to send off the soldiers. It was a way to show our support and wish them long life. Sometimes the soldiers’ parents would give us tangerines as a thank you, which made me very happy as a child.
The transcript does not mention anything about picture brides, so I have no personal story or experience related to that event.
The transcript does not include any direct experiences or stories about Hawaii, so I cannot share anything about it from my life.

I recall a white man, likely an American, who came to our town and showed us a story about Jesus using magic lantern slides. This was a rare and memorable event for me as a child in Hiroshima Prefecture. Although I was raised Buddhist, that encounter with a story from the United States left a lasting impression on my heart.
